risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those eligible were listed in alphabetical order by surname. They were then selected randomly from the patient list, using a computer-generated random numbers table." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random method for selecting participants, and later states that the 64 consenting families were "randomly assigned" to the two groups. The use of computer-generated random numbers supports truly random allocation sequence generation.

Quote: "Written consent was obtained in a face-to-face interview and the participants were then asked by the principal researcher to draw a sealed opaque envelope, in which a number card indicated to which group they had been allocated (1 = psycho-education group and 2 = routine care group)." Comment: This explicitly describes a sequential participant-level allocation revealed via sealed opaque envelopes, which is a concealment mechanism that prevents foreknowledge of the upcoming assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Except for the principal researcher and the group instructor, all other clinic staffs were blinded to treatment allocation." Comment: Participants and the personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded: caregivers were asked to draw an envelope showing their allocation, and the psychoeducation group was delivered by the principal researcher/group instructor, so those delivering and receiving the intervention knew group assignment. Because the outcomes included subjective caregiver-reported measures (e.g., burden, family functioning) and patient functioning that could plausibly be influenced by awareness of allocation, this creates a high risk of performance bias across the study overall.

Quote: "An independent assessor (a research assistant) was trained to undertake measurements at Time 1, 2 and 3 using a set of questionnaires." Comment: Although an independent assessor collected some data, the main outcomes included caregiver-completed questionnaires (FBIS, FAD, FSSI) and the outcome measurement was therefore patient/family self-report, which is highly susceptible to knowledge of allocation because participants were not blinded to the psychoeducation/support intervention. In addition, patient functioning (SLOF) was also assessed via questionnaires, so the domain is judged on the most vulnerable subjective outcomes rather than the objective hospitalization record data.

Quote: "Analysis of data was on an intention-to-treat basis [35]. All subjects, who completed the intervention or not, were followed up over a 12-month post-intervention period." Comment: The paper reports very low attrition and states that all randomized subjects were followed regardless of completion status. It further specifies only 5 dropouts/absentees across 68 participants (psychoeducation group n = 3; routine care group n = 2), which is about 7.4% overall and balanced across arms (~8.8% vs ~5.9%, a difference of ~2.9 percentage points). This is below the thresholds for concern, and there is no indication that missing outcome data were substantial or differentially related to outcomes.
